title: Diagnose the latency
intro: An API's p99 latency spiked to 900 ms under load, even though the average looks fine. Open the clues, then diagnose the real bottleneck.

facts.label: Average latency
facts.value: Reassuring: mean is ~40 ms. But p99 is 900 ms and climbing with traffic.

facts.label: CPU profile
facts.value: The request handler itself computes in ~6 ms. CPU on each box sits at ~30%.

facts.label: Under load
facts.value: Latency was flat until ~7k req/s, then rose sharply — a clear knee.

facts.label: Request queue
facts.value: Time spent waiting in the queue before processing grows fast once traffic climbs.

facts.label: Topology
facts.value: One large vertically-scaled instance behind the load balancer; no horizontal replicas.

question: What's really causing the p99 spike?
choices: The algorithm is too slow — optimise the handler's code
Waiting time dominates: the system is past its capacity knee and needs to scale out
The average proves performance is fine; the p99 alert is a false alarm
The database is the bottleneck — add an index
debrief: Response time = processing + waiting. Processing is only ~6 ms and CPU is idle-ish, so the code isn't the problem — waiting time is ~88% of the latency. Traffic crossed the capacity knee, so requests pile up in the queue. The fix is capacity/concurrency — scale out horizontally with more replicas — not micro-optimising a handler that's already fast. And the low average is exactly why you SLA on p99: it hid the tail entirely.
